text: Kubernetes is wildly over-engineered for 90% of the teams running it. You don't need a distributed control plane, etcd quorum, and a service mesh to run three stateless web services — two VMs behind a load balancer would be more reliable and a tenth of the ops burden.
label: opinion_or_critique

text: I don't buy the 'serverless is always cheaper' line. The moment you have steady traffic a reserved instance demolishes Lambda on cost, and you drop the cold-start headaches too. It only wins for spiky, low-volume workloads.

text: If your Postgres query jumped from 5ms to 2s overnight, it's probably a plan flip: once the table grew past a threshold the planner's row estimate crossed the cost crossover and it switched from an index scan to a sequential scan. Run ANALYZE and check pg_stat_statements to confirm.
label: technical_insight
notes: synthetic boundary example — authored during label audit; NOT scraped

text: TCP slow start is why the first few requests on a fresh connection feel laggy. The congestion window starts small and roughly doubles each round trip until it hits ssthresh, so a new connection literally can't use the full bandwidth yet. That's what HTTP/2 multiplexing and connection reuse are working around.
label: technical_insight
notes: synthetic boundary example — authored during label audit; NOT scraped

text: Generational garbage collectors are fast because of the weak generational hypothesis: most objects die young. So the GC only traces the small young generation on most cycles and rarely walks the whole heap, which is why allocation-heavy code can still have short pause times.
label: technical_insight
notes: synthetic boundary example — authored during label audit; NOT scraped

text: A Bloom filter can never give a false negative because it only ever sets bits, never clears them. A lookup that finds any required bit unset is a guaranteed miss. False positives happen when unrelated inserts have already set every bit a missing key hashes to.
label: technical_insight
notes: synthetic boundary example — authored during label audit; NOT scraped

text: Adding an index speeds up reads but slows down writes, because every INSERT now has to update the B-tree as well as the heap. That's why bolting an index onto a write-heavy table can tank overall throughput even though your SELECTs got faster — it's a direct read/write trade-off.
label: technical_insight
notes: synthetic boundary example — authored during label audit; NOT scraped

text: 0.1 can't be stored exactly in a float because it's binary floating point, and 0.1 is a repeating fraction in base 2 the same way 1/3 repeats in base 10. The tiny rounding error left over is exactly why 0.1 + 0.2 doesn't equal 0.3 in most languages.
label: technical_insight
notes: synthetic boundary example — authored during label audit; NOT scraped

text: HTTPS doesn't hide which site you're visiting from the network: the SNI field is sent in the clear during the TLS handshake, so an observer sees the hostname even though the page contents are encrypted. Encrypted Client Hello (ECH) is the mechanism being rolled out to close that gap.
label: technical_insight
notes: synthetic boundary example — authored during label audit; NOT scraped

text: Hash maps degrade toward O(n) when too many keys land in the same bucket. Most implementations resize and rehash once the load factor passes ~0.75 to keep the average chain short — that occasional full rehash is why one unlucky insert can briefly spike in latency.
label: technical_insight
